landing.feature1Desc: Discover your cognitive channel, strategy, direction, medium, and logical form.
landing.feature2Desc: Identify where you lose information when translating thought into communication.
landing.feature3Desc: Get personalized tips on how to communicate better with ChatGPT, Claude, and other AI.
landing.info: 30 scenario-based questions. About 10-15 minutes. Based on neuroscience and cognitive psychology research.

quiz.instruction: For each scenario, choose the two answers that best represent you. If only one strongly resonates, pick just that one.

A1.text: You're trying to understand a new, complex concept. As you think it through, what happens inside your head?
A1.a: I talk to myself internally: I build sentences, argue with myself, silently raise objections
A1.b: I see images, diagrams, spatial arrangements — the concept takes visual shape before it becomes words
A1.c: I feel a physical sensation: tension, openness, bodily discomfort that tells me if I'm on the right track
A1.d: The concept has a "rhythm" or a "melody" — I can tell when something clashes or feels harmonious
A1.e: There's nothing specific: no words, no images, no sensations. It's pure, abstract reasoning with no form

A2.text: You wake up in the middle of the night with the solution to a problem you'd been mulling over. What form does it take?
A2.a: As a sentence or an argument — "of course, the point is that..."
A2.b: As a mental image or diagram — I "see" the solution
A2.c: As a bodily certainty — I "feel" it's right before I can even explain it
A2.d: As a sense that "everything clicks" — a balance, almost musical
A2.e: As a sudden knowing without form — I know the answer but can't tell how I'm thinking it

A3.text: You need to explain how a complex system works (an organization, software, an ecosystem) to someone. In your mind, how is that system represented?
A3.a: As a narrative: "first X happens, then Y reacts, so Z changes..."
A3.b: As a map or diagram with components linked by arrows and relationships
A3.c: As something I "feel" working — I sense the tensions between the parts
A3.d: As a rhythm or flow — some parts are fast, others slow, some in sync
A3.e: As a set of abstract rules and relationships, with no need to visualize it

A4.text: You're listening to someone present an idea. At some point you sense something doesn't add up. How do you notice it?
A4.a: I mentally form the objection: "yes, but that contradicts what you said earlier..."
A4.b: I "see" a puzzle piece that doesn't fit, a picture that doesn't line up
A4.c: I feel physical discomfort: something in my stomach, a tension, an urge to interrupt
A4.d: The argument "sounds off" — like a wrong note in a melody
A4.e: I spot the logical flaw without it taking any specific shape

A5.text: You have an important decision to make. You take a moment to reflect. What do you actually do?
A5.a: I have an inner dialogue, listing pros and cons in words inside my head
A5.b: I visually imagine the alternative scenarios, like scenes from a film
A5.c: I ask myself "how do I feel about this option?" and listen to the sensations
A5.d: I look for which option "sounds right" — the right rhythm, the right tone
A5.e: I reason through the logical implications of each option without involving senses or emotions

B1.text: You have an idea that seems good. What's your next step?
B1.a: I share it with someone I know will tear it apart — I want to see where it breaks
B1.b: I ask myself questions: "why do I think this? What assumptions am I making? What am I not considering?"
B1.c: I put it into practice in rough form, then refine it: first attempt, then improve
B1.d: I try to explain it to someone (or to myself) as if I had to teach it
B1.e: I compare it with similar cases: "in other similar contexts, how did it work out?"

B2.text: You're facing a system or process you don't understand. How do you approach it?
B2.a: I take it apart piece by piece: I remove components until I understand what each one does
B2.b: I gather information without rushing — I read, observe, accumulate, until something clicks
B2.c: I eliminate wrong explanations one by one until the right one remains
B2.d: I try to translate it into a language or framework I already know
B2.e: I look for patterns: "this reminds me of something I've seen before"

B3.text: You're in a meeting where a difficult decision is being discussed. What's your typical contribution?
B3.a: I build the counter-argument to the prevailing view, even if I don't actually believe it
B3.b: I generate as many alternatives as possible without judging them
B3.c: I try to put myself in the shoes of the people involved: "how would the client see this? And the supplier?"
B3.d: I step back and ask "are we reasoning the right way? Are we using the right approach for this type of problem?"
B3.e: I turn the discussion into a story: "imagine that a year from now it went like this..."

B4.text: A project you're working on isn't going as planned. How do you react?
B4.a: I imagine the project has already completely failed and reconstruct the causes in reverse
B4.b: I try to compete with the problem: I treat it as an opponent to beat, a challenge
B4.c: I go back to the basic version and iterate: strip away the extras, try again, adjust
B4.d: I ask myself what my gut says — I've seen enough similar problems to have a "trained eye"
B4.e: I try to take the problem apart and figure out which piece isn't working

B5.text: You need to learn about a new topic you know little about. How do you study?
B5.a: I read and gather material without trying to understand right away — I accumulate until a structure emerges
B5.b: I try to explain what I've understood (even poorly) to someone or to myself right away
B5.c: I look for something similar I already know: "it's like..."
B5.d: I ask progressive questions: what? why? how? what would happen if...?
B5.e: I translate everything into a framework I know (e.g., a biology problem read in economic terms)

B6.text: Someone proposes a solution that feels wrong to you, but you don't know why. How do you proceed?
B6.a: I argue against it, even without having an alternative yet — the critique will bring the problem to light
B6.b: I mentally build the scenario where the solution gets implemented and look for where it fails
B6.c: I ask myself: "what hidden assumption is this person making?"
B6.d: I try to see it from the proposer's perspective: "why do they see it this way?"
B6.e: I rule out the reasons it could be right one by one, to isolate the problem

B7.text: How would you describe your relationship with rules and constraints in a creative project?
B7.a: Constraints help me: time limits, format, resources — I think better within boundaries
B7.b: I generate freely first, then apply constraints as a filter
B7.c: I treat them as game rules: part of the fun is finding the solution within the limits
B7.d: I question them: "is this constraint really necessary?"
B7.e: I translate them into formal language: equations, code, explicit rules

C1.text: You're trying to solve a problem. How do you go about it?
C1.a: I generate as many different options as possible before choosing — I explore broadly
C1.b: I quickly narrow down the options and eliminate the least promising ones — I get straight to the point
C1.c: I look for a completely different angle from the obvious one — a radical shift in perspective
C1.d: I alternate: first I expand the possibilities, then I select, then I expand again

C2.text: Someone presents you with a specific case and asks for your opinion. Your first reaction is:
C2.a: I look for the general rule: "this case is an instance of which broader principle?"
C2.b: I look for other similar concrete cases: "when has something like this happened before?"
C2.c: I ask myself "how will this look in 5 years? What if it had happened 10 years ago?"
C2.d: I look for what to remove: "what is the essence of this case, stripped of everything unnecessary?"

C3.text: You need to improve something that already works (a process, a product, a presentation). How do you tackle it?
C3.a: I add: new features, new steps, new details
C3.b: I remove: I eliminate unnecessary steps, simplify, reduce to the minimum
C3.c: I compress: I try to get the same result in less space/time/words
C3.d: I change the frame: I look at it from a completely different angle to see what I was missing

C4.text: A friend asks you for advice on a personal problem. What's your first mental move?
C4.a: I look for the principle: "the underlying problem here is..."
C4.b: I ask for concrete details: "tell me about a specific episode"
C4.c: I project into the future: "where does this situation lead in a year if you change nothing?"
C4.d: I generate options: "you could do A, or B, or C, or..."

C5.text: How do you judge the quality of an explanation?
C5.a: It's good if it covers all cases and exceptions — completeness
C5.b: It's good if it gets to the point quickly — efficiency
C5.c: It's good if it fits in one sentence — compactness
C5.d: It's good if it works at different levels — from the specific case to the general principle

D1.text: You're working on a complex problem alone. What do you do to get unstuck?
D1.a: I talk about it out loud, even if no one is there
D1.b: I open a document and start writing out my reasoning
D1.c: I grab paper and pen and draw a diagram, a map, a chart
D1.d: I build something: a prototype, a model, even just with objects on the desk
D1.e: I write pseudocode, equations, or translate the problem into a formal language

D2q.text: You need to explain a complex idea to someone. How do you present it?
D2q.a: I tell them about it verbally, reasoning together
D2q.b: I write a text: email, document, structured message
D2q.c: I draw a diagram or chart on a whiteboard
D2q.d: I give a demo or build a prototype: "look, it works like this"
D2q.e: I formalize it: specs, formulas, code, models

D3q.text: Who do you mainly externalize your thoughts for?
D3q.a: For myself: I keep notes, journals, memos that I reread
D3q.b: For the people I work with: I communicate a lot, explain, discuss
D3q.c: For my future self: blog posts, documentation, recordings I'll use later
D3q.d: For systems: prompts for AI, code, formal specifications

D4q.text: Think back to a time you had an important insight. How did you "capture" it so you wouldn't lose it?
D4q.a: I said it out loud to someone right away
D4q.b: I took written notes as soon as possible
D4q.c: I drew a quick sketch or diagram
D4q.d: I put it into practice immediately, even in rough form
D4q.e: I formalized it: a formula, an algorithm, a model

D5q.text: Which tool would you miss the most if it were taken away?
D5q.a: The ability to talk to someone (or to talk out loud to myself)
D5q.c: The ability to draw/diagram (whiteboard, paper, tablet)
D5q.e: The ability to formalize (code, formulas, models)

E1.text: A colleague tells you: "Our best clients are all in the manufacturing sector." How do you react mentally?
E1.a: "So we should focus on manufacturing" — the rule is clear, I apply the conclusion
E1.b: "Interesting, but how many clients have we looked at? Is the sample large enough?" — I check if the generalization holds
E1.c: "Why manufacturing specifically? What do these clients have in common that explains the success?" — I look for the underlying explanation
E1.d: "This reminds me of what happens in sector X: their best clients were also all in one segment..." — I look for analogies

E2.text: You need to predict whether a new initiative will work. How do you build your prediction?
E2.a: I start from principles: "if A holds and B holds, then necessarily..."
E2.b: I look at historical data: "the last 5 times we did something similar, it went like this..."
E2.c: I ask myself: "what's the most plausible scenario, considering everything I know?"
E2.d: I look for an analogous case in another context: "in sector Y they did something similar and..."

E3.text: You encounter an unexpected result. Your first reaction?
E3.a: "One of the premises must be wrong — let's review them one by one"
E3.b: "A single case isn't enough — let's see if it happens again"
E3.c: "What's the most likely explanation for this result?"
E3.d: "Where have I seen something like this before? What does this relate to?"

E4.text: How do you tend to persuade someone?
E4.a: With logic: "if you accept X, then you must accept Y, because it follows necessarily"
E4.b: With evidence: "look, in all these cases it happened this way"
E4.c: With explanation: "the most likely reason this happens is..."

F1.text: Think of a time when you understood something but couldn't explain it. What happened?
F1.a: I "saw" it in my mind but couldn't put it into words
F1.b: I "felt" it was right but couldn't argue for it logically
F1.c: I had it clear verbally but couldn't turn it into a diagram
F1.d: That doesn't happen to me often — I can usually translate between formats

F2.text: When you communicate with an LLM (ChatGPT, Claude, etc.), do you ever feel frustrated because...
F2.a: I'd like to draw a diagram but I can only type text
F2.b: I'd rather discuss it out loud — writing prompts slows me down
F2.c: My insights are "feelings" I can't turn into prompts
F2.d: I don't find it particularly frustrating — I'm comfortable communicating in writing

F3.text: In which situation do you feel your thinking loses quality?
F3.a: When I have to write instead of talk
F3.b: When I have to talk instead of write
F3.c: When I have to explain in words what's a picture or diagram in my head
F3.d: When I have to formalize something I "feel" is right
F3.e: When I can't build or prototype and can only describe

1_1.name: Inner Verbal
1_1.risk: Over-verbalization: words can mask gaps in reasoning
1_1.llm: Write prompts the way you'd speak. You can be conversational — LLMs handle natural language well.

1_2.name: Visuo-spatial
1_2.strength: Big-picture view, connections between concepts, design
1_2.risk: Difficulty linearizing: turning a map into a sequential argument
1_2.llm: Describe the structure you have in mind: "Imagine a table with...", "Think of a tree where the root node is...". Use spatial relationships.

1_3.name: Somatic
1_3.risk: Difficulty justifying: "I feel it" doesn't convince in a meeting
1_3.llm: Make your intuitions explicit: "I have a gut feeling that X is the right direction — help me find out if there are objective reasons for it." Ask the LLM to translate intuition into reasoning.

1_4.name: Sonic / Rhythmic
1_4.strength: Sensitivity to timing, narrative structure, balance
1_4.risk: Rare: few work environments explicitly value it
1_4.llm: Use rhythm in your structure: lists, parallelisms, alternations. Ask for responses with rhythmic structure.

1_5.name: Abstract-propositional
1_5.risk: Detachment from the concrete: risk of building castles in the air
1_5.llm: You can use formal language, pseudocode, logical notation. LLMs understand it well.

2_1.name: Adversarial
2_1.risk: Others may perceive you as confrontational
2_1.llm: Ask the LLM to play devil's advocate: "Find the 3 weakest points of this idea." Or: "Argue against this position as strongly as possible."

2_2.name: Socratic
2_2.risk: Slowness; may seem evasive ("just answer instead of asking!")
2_2.llm: Ask the LLM to ask you questions instead of giving answers: "Don't give me the solution — ask me the right questions to find it."

2_3.name: Iterative
2_3.strength: Pragmatism, ability to start with little
2_3.llm: Work in multiple turns: "Give me a first draft, then we'll refine it." Don't aim for the perfect answer on the first prompt.

2_4.name: Teaching
2_4.risk: Dependence on an interlocutor (real or imagined)
2_4.llm: Ask the LLM to explain as if to a beginner, then try to re-explain it yourself. Or: "Ask me the questions a student would ask about this topic."

2_5.name: Deconstructive
2_5.llm: "Break this down into all its components." "What happens if I remove X?"

2_6.name: Accumulation
2_6.risk: Looks like inaction from the outside; risk of never reaching the threshold
2_6.llm: Feed the LLM information in stages and ask it to help you spot when a structure emerges from the accumulated material.

2_7.name: Elimination
2_7.risk: May discard valid options too early
2_7.llm: Ask the LLM to list all possible explanations, then eliminate them one by one with reasoning.

2_8.name: Analogy
2_8.risk: Wrong analogies: surface similarity passed off as structural
2_8.llm: "What does this problem resemble in another context?" "Find me an analogy for..."

2_9.name: Internal Contradiction
2_9.risk: Paralysis: the inner devil's advocate never stops
2_9.llm: Ask the LLM to steelman both sides: "Give me the strongest argument for and against this position."

2_10.name: Generative
2_10.risk: Difficulty converging: too many options, no choice
2_10.llm: "Give me 10 different approaches to this problem, including unconventional ones."

2_11.name: Translation
2_11.risk: Risk of forcing the wrong framework
2_11.llm: Ask the LLM to reframe the problem in a different domain or language you're more familiar with.

2_12.name: Narrative
2_12.risk: Risk of narrativizing everything, even where analysis is needed
2_12.llm: "Tell me the story of how..." "Explain it as a narrative with a protagonist and a conflict."

2_13.name: Simulation
2_13.risk: Risk of paralysis from negative scenarios
2_13.llm: "Imagine this project has already failed — what were the most likely causes?" (Pre-mortem technique.)

2_14.name: Pattern Recognition
2_14.risk: False positives: seeing patterns that aren't there
2_14.llm: "Have you seen similar patterns in other contexts? What usually happens next?"

2_15.name: Competition
2_15.risk: Dependence on having an opponent; not everything is a contest
2_15.llm: Frame the problem as a challenge: "What's the most elegant solution you can find?" Use the LLM as a sparring partner.

2_16.name: Metacognitive
2_16.risk: Risk of meta-loop: thinking about thinking without actually thinking
2_16.llm: "Before answering, tell me which approach you'd use for this problem and why."

2_17.name: Perspective-taking
2_17.risk: Risk of losing your own position
2_17.llm: "Analyze this from the perspective of [stakeholder X]. What would they prioritize?"

3_1.name: Divergent
3_1.llm: "Give me 10 different approaches to this problem, including unconventional ones."

3_2.name: Convergent
3_2.llm: "Among these options, which is the best and why? Be direct."

3_3.name: Lateral
3_3.risk: Instability: switching frame before fully exploring the current one
3_3.llm: "Reframe this problem from a completely different angle."

3_4.name: Abstractive
3_4.llm: "What general principle underlies this specific case?"

3_5.name: Concretizing
3_5.llm: "Give me a concrete example of how this principle works in practice."

3_6.name: Subtractive
3_6.llm: "What can I remove from this solution without losing the essentials?"

3_7.name: Compressive
3_7.llm: "Summarize it in one sentence." "What's the core?"

3_8.name: Temporal Scale
3_8.risk: Risk of deferring action to the "long term"
3_8.llm: "How will this look in 5 years? What if we'd done it 10 years ago?"

4_1.name: Voice
4_1.llm: Use voice-to-text tools to dictate your prompts naturally, then refine.

4_2.name: Writing
4_2.llm: You're naturally suited to LLM interaction. Write structured prompts and iterate on the text.

4_3.name: Diagram
4_3.risk: Not everyone understands diagrams; linearization is often needed
4_3.llm: Describe your diagrams in text: "Imagine a flowchart where..." Use spatial metaphors to convey structure.

4_4.name: Gesture / Prototype
4_4.llm: Ask the LLM to generate code or mockups you can immediately build on. Use it for rapid prototyping.

4_5.name: Code / Formal
4_5.llm: You can use formal language, pseudocode, or logical notation directly. LLMs understand it well.

5_1.name: Deductive
5_1.risk: Rigidity: if the premises are wrong, everything collapses
5_1.llm: "Give me a step-by-step reasoning with explicit premises."

5_2.name: Inductive
5_2.llm: "Show me 5 examples of X and then generalize the pattern."

5_3.name: Abductive
5_3.llm: "Given these facts, what's the most plausible hypothesis? And what are the alternatives?"

5_4.name: Analogical
5_4.risk: Wrong analogies; correlation mistaken for causation
5_4.llm: "In what other domain does a structurally similar problem exist?"

visual_to_verbal.desc: You think in images and spatial layouts, but externalize through speech or writing
visual_to_verbal.strategy: Use spatial metaphors ("it's above", "it connects to", "the center is..."). Describe the diagram you see in your mind before building your argument. With an LLM: "Imagine a diagram where..."

somatic_to_verbal.desc: You think through physical sensations, but externalize through speech, writing, or code
somatic_to_verbal.strategy: Translate feelings into scales: "from 1 to 10, how convinced am I" before making your case. Use body language: "this solution gives me energy / weighs me down." With an LLM: "I have a strong intuition that X is right — help me understand why."

verbal_to_diagram.desc: Your reasoning is sequential, but you need to produce simultaneous visual output
verbal_to_diagram.strategy: Start from an ordered list, then transform it into a diagram. Your natural flow is linear — use that as scaffolding for visual output.

abstract_to_voice.desc: Your thinking is dense and compressed, but speech requires linearity
abstract_to_voice.strategy: Prepare a mental outline before speaking. Use bridge phrases: "the key point is..." to pace your delivery.

sonic_to_writing.desc: Your thinking has a rhythm that writing can lose
sonic_to_writing.strategy: Try writing the way you would speak, then structure it afterward. Let the rhythm come first, the form later.

verbal_to_writing.strategy: Write fast, revise later. Don't try to edit while writing — capture the flow first.

visual_to_diagram.desc: Natural alignment between how you think and how you externalize
visual_to_diagram.strategy: No compensation needed — you're in your element. Focus on making your diagrams readable for others.

verbal_to_voice.desc: Natural alignment between how you think and how you externalize
verbal_to_voice.strategy: No compensation needed — you're in your element. Focus on structuring your speech for your audience.

somatic_to_formal.desc: You think through intuition and bodily sensations, but need to produce formalized output
somatic_to_formal.strategy: Use an intermediate step: first turn the feeling into words, then formalize the words. Ask an LLM to help bridge from intuition to structure.

gesture_to_verbal.desc: You think by building and prototyping, but need to express through words
gesture_to_verbal.strategy: Describe what you'd build step by step: "First I'd take this piece, connect it to that..." Use the construction process as your narrative.

results.title: Your Thinking Profile
results.llmStrategies: How to talk to an LLM
results.gapIntro: The gap between your cognitive channel and your externalization medium shows where you lose information when communicating.
results.llmIntro: Based on your profile, here's how to structure your prompts to get the most out of an LLM.

share.title: Share your profile
share.text: I just discovered my thinking profile!

about.title: About
about.intro: This project started with a question: how do we actually think? Not how well we think, but what shape our thinking takes.
about.modelDesc: We identified 5 independent dimensions that describe a person's thinking profile:
about.papersDesc: The model is grounded in peer-reviewed research from neuroscience, cognitive psychology, and philosophy of mind.
